summary: I was drafted into the Service but chose to enlist in the United States Marine Corps during World War II. The Marine Corps had fewer people waiting to sign up, so that influenced my decision. I went through eight weeks of basic training and served primarily in the South Pacific with the 4th Marine Division. My time in the Marines was challenging but left a lasting impression, especially from my drill instructor Corporal Eisenberg.

Before joining the Marine Corps, I was living in Sewanee, Tennessee. I was attending prep school there, specifically St. Andrew's-Sewanee School. It was a quiet place compared to where I ended up serving during the war. Sewanee was really my home before the military called me away.

I was going to St. Andrew's-Sewanee School, a prep school in Tennessee, before I was drafted. It was there that I was living and studying before the war interrupted my education. After my service, I returned to continue my studies elsewhere. That school was part of my life before the Marine Corps.

After basic training in San Diego, my unit was formed at Camp Pendleton in Oceanside, California. We did extensive training there before heading into combat. Camp Pendleton was where the 4th Marine Division was reinforced and prepared for the battles ahead. It was also where I spent some liberty time before shipping out.

Oceanside, California is where Camp Pendleton is located, and that's where I was stationed after boot camp. We trained there extensively before shipping out to the South Pacific. It was a place of transition from training to combat for me and my fellow Marines.

I served with the 4th Marine Division during World War II, which started with about 17,000 men. We were reinforced with other units when formed at Camp Pendleton. The division saw combat in several key battles including Roi-Namur, Saipan, Tinian, and Iwo Jima. We suffered losses but also earned honors like the Presidential Unit Citation.

I went through my basic training in San Diego before moving on to Camp Pendleton. San Diego was the first place I experienced military life in the Marine Corps. It was the starting point for my journey into the war.

After training at Camp Pendleton, we boarded ship in San Francisco to head out to the South Pacific. San Francisco was the last U.S. port we saw before combat. It was a significant departure point for us Marines going into battle.

My first combat experience was at Roi-Namur in the Kwajalein Atoll in the South Pacific. We landed there after sailing from San Francisco and stopping briefly at Pearl Harbor. Roi-Namur was part of the initial battles that tested our division in the war.

Kwajalein Atoll was where Roi-Namur is located, and it was one of the first places I saw combat. We trained hard before landing there, and it was a key strategic location in the South Pacific. The atoll was part of the island-hopping campaign during the war.

I served primarily in the South Pacific during World War II, participating in battles across several islands. The South Pacific was a vast and challenging theater of war, including places like Kwajalein, Saipan, Tinian, and Iwo Jima. It was where I experienced the realities of combat and military life.

We stopped briefly at Pearl Harbor on our way to Roi-Namur, but none of the enlisted men were allowed off the ship. I could see the evidence of the attack that had happened there before our time. It was a sobering reminder of the war we were entering.

I fought in the Battle of Saipan as part of the 4th Marine Division. It was one of the major battles in the Pacific campaign and was tough fighting. For Saipan and Tinian, our unit was awarded a Presidential Unit Citation, which was a proud moment for all of us.

The Battle of Tinian was another key fight I was involved in during the war. It followed Saipan and was part of the island-hopping strategy. I was wounded there and received a Purple Heart for a shrapnel injury from a mortar while we were dug in near an airstrip.

I also served in the Battle of Iwo Jima, one of the most famous and brutal battles in the Pacific. It was a tough fight with heavy casualties, but our division played a crucial role. I was proud to be part of that effort alongside my fellow Marines.

Our unit was awarded the Presidential Unit Citation for the Saipan-Tinian invasion. It was an honor that recognized the bravery and effectiveness of the 4th Marine Division in those battles. Receiving that citation was a highlight of my military service.

I was awarded the Purple Heart after being wounded by mortar shrapnel at Tinian. It happened one morning when we were dug in near an airstrip, and I was evacuated to an aid station but returned to my unit soon after. The Purple Heart is a reminder of the sacrifice and danger we faced in combat.

While stationed in the Pacific, I had the chance to visit Hawaii once during my service. I was stationed on Maui, which is about 60 miles from the big island of Hawaii. We went over to Honolulu for R and R at the Royal Hawaiian Hotel, which was a welcome break from combat duties.

I was stationed on Maui for a time during the war, which was a quieter place compared to the combat zones. While there, I had liberty to visit local towns like Wailuku and spend time at the beach. It was a peaceful refuge amid the intensity of the war.

I visited Honolulu once while on R and R from Maui. We stayed at the Royal Hawaiian Hotel, and I was appointed billeting NCO to organize our quarters. It was a rare chance to enjoy some comforts and the beach during the war.

During a rest and relaxation trip from Maui, I stayed at the Royal Hawaiian Hotel in Honolulu with about 21 other Marines. I was the billeting NCO, responsible for organizing our quarters. The hotel had bunk beds and access to beach equipment, which was a nice change from military life.

We traveled to Honolulu on a Landing Craft Infantry, or LCI, during our R and R from Maui. I found the fellows' quarters on the LCI and helped set up when we arrived. The LCI was an important vessel for moving troops between islands in the Pacific.

I have a close friend from boot camp who lives in Chattanooga, Tennessee. We managed to keep in touch over the years and see each other at Marine Corps veterans' gatherings. Chattanooga is where I often reconnect with Leon Lewis, another Marine buddy.

Another Marine friend I served with lives in Texas now, although his health isn't very good. We don't see each other as often as I'd like, but we remain connected through memories of our service. Texas is part of the network of old comrades from the war.

Leon Lewis is a Marine buddy from boot camp who lives in Chattanooga, Tennessee. We went through basic training around the same time and kept in touch after the war. I see him occasionally at Marine Corps veterans' reunions, and those meetings are always meaningful.

I was discharged from the Marine Corps at Camp Lejeune on October 31st, 1943. I remember the crowded bus ride after discharge, sitting over the engine that got pretty hot. Camp Lejeune marked the end of my military service and the beginning of my return to civilian life.

After the war, I enrolled at the University of the South, where I met my future wife during summer school. The university wasn't co-ed at the time except for day students in summer sessions. Returning to school was an important step in rebuilding my life after the Marine Corps.

My education after the war was supported by the GI Bill, which helped many veterans like me attend college. It made it possible to go back to school and start a new chapter in life after military service. The GI Bill was a crucial benefit for returning servicemen.

While stationed at Camp Pendleton, my friend Otto and I went to the Hollywood Canteen during liberty. There we saw celebrities like Mickey Rooney and Maria Montez. I even got to dance briefly with Maria Montez, which was a memorable experience amidst the war.

At the Hollywood Canteen, I saw Mickey Rooney perform and learned he was quite the drummer. It was surprising to see such a famous actor showing his musical talent. That visit was a bright spot during our time at Camp Pendleton.

During a visit to the Hollywood Canteen, I got to dance a couple of steps with Maria Montez before someone else cut in. She was one of the celebrities entertaining servicemen at the time. It was a fun and unexpected moment during the war.

While stationed in Maui, there was an orchestra leader who played, but I can't recall if it was Tommy Dorsey. We didn't have much entertainment, so these musical events stood out. Tommy Dorsey was a famous bandleader, but I just can't remember if it was him exactly.

During basic training, the song 'Old Black Magic' was played constantly over the loudspeaker. It became a kind of theme music for those days when we weren't running drills. That tune really stuck with me as part of the Marine Corps experience.
